Walk through: left=0, right=n-1, leftMax=0, rightMax=0. For height=[0,1,0,2,1,0,1,3,2,1,2,1] answer is 6. Code it in Java and explain why two pointers work.

17. Pair Difference (Optimized): Given an unsorted integer array and target K, find all unique pairs with |a-b|=K using a HashSet in O(n). Handle edge cases: K=0 (duplicate pairs), negative numbers, duplicates in array. Example: arr=[1,7,5,9,2,12,3], K=2 → [(1,3),(3,5),(5,7),(7,9)]. Code in Java with HashSet approach.

18. LeetCode 39 - Combination Sum (Backtracking): Given a list of distinct candidates and a target, find all unique combinations where chosen numbers sum to target. Candidates can be reused. Example: candidates=[2,3,6,7], target=7 → [[2,2,3],[7]]. Implement in Java using backtracking. Explain the recursion tree, how you avoid duplicate combinations, and time complexity O(N^(T/M)) where T=target, M=min candidate.

19. N-Queens Problem (Introduction): Place N queens on an N×N chessboard so that no two queens attack each other (no same row, column, or diagonal). For N=4, there are 2 solutions. Explain the backtracking approach: place queen row by row, check if column and both diagonals are safe before placing. Write the isSafe() method in Java that checks: (a) same column above, (b) upper-left diagonal, (c) upper-right diagonal. What is the time complexity? O(N!).

15. Trapping Rainwater (Full Analysis): Solve LeetCode #42 with all three approaches: (1) Brute force O(n^2)/O(1), (2) Prefix/Suffix max arrays O(n)/O(n), (3) Two-pointer O(n)/O(1). Code all three in Java. Then extend: if heights can change dynamically (point updates), how would you use a Segment Tree to answer range trapped-water queries efficiently? Discuss time/space complexity of each approach.

16. Permutations with Backtracking (Advanced): Implement LeetCode #46 - Permutations in Java using backtracking. Given nums=[1,2,3], return all 6 permutations. Use a boolean[] visited array and a List<Integer> path. Then solve LeetCode #47 - Permutations II with duplicates (nums=[1,1,2]) using sorting + skip-duplicate pruning in backtracking. Explain how to prune: if nums[i]==nums[i-1] && !visited[i-1], skip. Analyze time complexity O(n * n!) and space O(n).

17. Combination Sum Complete Set (Senior): Solve all four LeetCode Combination Sum variants in Java: (1) #39 - reuse allowed, (2) #40 - each number once, duplicates in input, (3) #216 - exactly k numbers summing to n from 1-9, (4) #377 - count combinations (order matters, DP). For #39 and #40, implement backtracking with pruning. For #377, implement bottom-up DP. Compare when to use backtracking vs DP and the time complexities of each.

18. Pair Difference (Senior Design): Solve the pair difference problem at scale: Given a stream of 1 billion integers and a target K, find all pairs |a-b|=K. Design using: (1) External sort + two-pointer for disk-based processing, (2) Bloom filter for approximate membership, (3) Distributed approach using consistent hashing across nodes. Also implement the in-memory version using a sorted TreeSet: for each x, check if (x+K) or (x-K) exists. Handle duplicates and K=0 edge case. Time O(n log n), Space O(n).

19. N-Queens Problem (Complete Java Implementation - LeetCode #51 & #52): Implement the full N-Queens solution in Java using backtracking. Use a char[][] board initialized with '.', place 'Q' when safe. Write complete isSafe(board, row, col, n) checking: (1) column above rows 0..row-1, (2) upper-left diagonal, (3) upper-right diagonal. Recursive solve(board, row, n, result): if row==n add board snapshot to result, else try each col 0..n-1. Also solve #52 (count solutions only). For N=8 there are 92 solutions. Optimize using three boolean arrays (cols[], diag1[], diag2[]) for O(1) safety check. Code both the board-based and optimized bit-manipulation versions. Time: O(N!), Space: O(N^2). Explain why we only need to check upward diagonals and not downward.
